question.raw_question_text: A student investigates the vertical oscillations of a solid cylinder which floats in cooking oil. Fig. 1.1 shows a cylinder of radius r. The student places the cylinder of mass m in the oil. The cylinder is displaced vertically from its equilibrium position and released so that it oscillates. The period T of the oscillations is determined. A number of cylinders of different mass are available. It is suggested that the relationship between T and m is T = 2π√(m / (σKr^2)) where σ is the density of the oil and K is a constant. Design a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between T and m. Explain how your results could be used to determine a value for K. You should draw a diagram, on page 3, showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your account you should pay particular attention to: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: vertical oscillations of a solid cylinder floating in cooking oil
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: Mass of cylinder m is the independent variable and period T is the dependent variable.
Keep radius of cylinder constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: beaker with (cooking) oil on a bench or container supported by stand where stand is on a bench; cylinder partially submerged in (cooking) oil; cylinder and (cooking) oil labelled.
Method to determine mass m of cylinder, e.g. use a (top pan) balance.
Method to determine period or T, e.g. use a stopwatch / timer to time oscillations.
Method to determine diameter of cylinder, e.g. micrometer or calliper.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of T^2 against m.
Relationship valid if a straight line passing through the origin is produced.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Use gloves to prevent oil contacting skin / slippery hands OR perform experiment in a tray to prevent oil spillages.
Keep density of the oil constant.
Measure the mass of the beaker and oil, and measure the mass of the beaker. Calculate mass of oil = mass of the beaker and oil - mass of the beaker.
Measure the volume of the oil using a measuring cylinder. Calculate density using σ = mass / volume.
Time n oscillations and divide nT by n where n ≥ 5.
Use a fiducial mark to count oscillations.
Repeat experiment for each value of m and average T.
r = diameter / 2.
Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions and average.
Wait for oscillations to become even / steady.

target_answer.response:
Mass of cylinder m is the independent variable and period T is the dependent variable.
Keep radius of cylinder and density of the oil constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• beaker with (cooking) oil on a bench or container supported by stand where stand is on a bench
• cylinder partially submerged in (cooking) oil
• cylinder and (cooking) oil labelled.

Measure mass m of cylinder using a balance.
Measure time using a stopwatch. Wait for oscillations to become even / steady. Time n oscillations and divide nT by n, where n ≥ 5. Use a fiducial mark to count oscillations.
Measure diameter d of cylinder using caliper. Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions and average. Calculate r = d / 2.
For density of the oil, measure the mass of the beaker and oil, and measure the mass of the beaker. Calculate mass of oil = mass of the beaker and oil - mass of the beaker. Measure the volume of the oil using a measuring cylinder. Calculate density using σ = mass / volume.

Repeat experiment for each value of m and average T.
Plot a graph of T^2 against m.
Relationship valid if a straight line passing through the origin is produced.
K = 4π^2 / (σr^2 × gradient).

Safety:
Use gloves to prevent oil contacting skin / slippery hands or perform experiment in a tray to prevent oil spillages.

question.raw_question_text: A student investigates the current in a coil and a resistor connected in series, as shown in Fig. 1.1. The student connects a high-voltage d.c. power supply and a switch across the series combination. When the switch is closed, it takes time t for the current in the resistor of resistance R to reach a maximum value. The time t is a few milliseconds. There are a number of different unmarked resistors available. It is suggested that the relationship between t and R is t = KN^2A / LR where N is the number of turns of wire on the coil, A is the cross-sectional area of the coil, L is the length of the coil and K is a constant. Design a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between t and R. Explain how your results could be used to determine a value for K. You should draw a diagram, on page 3, showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your account you should pay particular attention to: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: current in a coil and a resistor connected in series with a high-voltage d.c. power supply
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: R is the independent variable and t is the dependent variable.
Keep the number of turns on the coil N constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram or correct symbols including: labelled d.c. power supply; switch in series with power supply, resistor and coil; complete workable circuit.
Circuit diagram to measure R, e.g. ammeter and voltmeter correctly positioned.
Method to determine time t, e.g. use a storage oscilloscope.
Method to determine A, e.g. measure diameter of the coil using calipers and calculate A = πd^2 / 4.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of t against 1 / R.
Relationship valid if a straight line passing through the origin is produced.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Switch off the high-voltage circuit before changing the resistor or touching components.
Wear insulating gloves to prevent electric shock.
Keep cross-sectional area A and length L of the coil constant.
Measure length L of the coil using a ruler or calipers.
Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions or at different points along the coil and average.
Measure the potential difference across the resistor and the current through it. Calculate R = V / I.
Repeat experiment for each value of R and average t.
Use the time-base of the oscilloscope to determine t.
Use smaller values of R to increase I.
To increase t, reduce L or increase N or increase A.

target_answer.response:
R is the independent variable and t is the dependent variable.
Keep number of turns on the coil N, cross-sectional area A and length L of the coil constant.

Labelled diagram or correct symbols including:
• labelled d.c. power supply
• switch in series with power supply, resistor and coil
• complete workable circuit
Draw a circuit diagram to measure R using an ammeter and voltmeter correctly positioned.

Measure diameter d of the coil using calipers. Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions or at different points along the coil and average. Calculate A = πd^2 / 4.
Measure length L of the coil using a ruler or calipers.
Measure the potential difference across the resistor and the current through it. Calculate R = V / I. Use smaller values of R to increase I.
Measure time t using a storage oscilloscope. Use the time-base of the oscilloscope to determine t.
To increase t, reduce L or increase N or increase A.

Repeat experiment for each value of R and average t.
Plot a graph of t against 1 / R.
Relationship valid if a straight line passing through the origin is produced.
K = gradient × L / (AN^2).

Safety:
Switch off the high-voltage circuit before changing the resistor or touching components.
Wear insulating gloves to prevent electric shock.

question.raw_question_text: A student investigates the heating of a solid metal cylinder. Fig. 1.1 shows the cylinder of cross-sectional area A and height h. The student places the cylinder and an electrical heater in a beaker of water. The electrical heater is switched on and the student measures the time t for the temperature of the water to increase by Δθ. A number of cylinders of the same material but with different cross-sectional areas are available. It is suggested that the relationship between t and A is Pt = AhWΔθ + ZΔθ where P is the power of the heater and W and Z are constants. Design a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between t and A. Explain how your results could be used to determine values for W and Z. You should draw a diagram, on page 3, showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your account you should pay particular attention to: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: heating of a solid metal cylinder in water using an electrical heater
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: A is the independent variable and t is the dependent variable.
Keep Δθ constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: beaker of water; cylinder in water; electrical heater in water; thermometer in water; minimum of three labels from heater, thermometer, cylinder, water, beaker.
Circuit diagram to determine power of the heater, e.g. ammeter and voltmeter correctly positioned with a power supply.
Method to determine time t, e.g. use a stopwatch.
Method to determine A, e.g. measure diameter of cylinder using calipers and calculate A = πd^2 / 4.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of t against A.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Use heatproof gloves to prevent burns from hot beaker, cylinder, heater or water.
Keep power P of the heater constant. Keep height h of the cylinder constant.
Keep initial temperature of the water constant.
Measure height h of the cylinder using a ruler or calipers.
Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions or at different positions along the cylinder and average.
Calculate power using P = VI.
Repeat measurements of t for the same A and average t.
Ensure the heater and cylinder are fully submerged in the water.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.
Insulate the beaker using a lid or foam around the outside.

target_answer.response:
A is the independent variable and t is the dependent variable.
Keep power P of the heater, height h of the cylinder and temperature increase Δθ constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• beaker of water
• cylinder in water
• electrical heater in water
• thermometer in water
• labels for the heater, thermometer, cylinder, water and beaker
Draw a circuit diagram with a power supply, ammeter and voltmeter connected to determine the power of the heater.

Measure diameter d of the cylinder using calipers. Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions or at different positions along the cylinder and average. Calculate A = πd^2 / 4.
Measure height h of the cylinder using a ruler or calipers.
Measure time t using a stopwatch.
Measure the current and potential difference of the heater and calculate P = VI.
Keep the initial temperature of the water constant. Ensure the heater and cylinder are fully submerged in the water. Insulate the beaker using a lid or foam around the outside.

Repeat measurements of t for the same A and average t.
Plot a graph of t against A.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.
W = gradient × hΔθ / P.
Z = y-intercept × Δθ / P.

Safety:
Use heatproof gloves to prevent burns from hot beaker, cylinder, heater or water.

question.raw_question_text: A student investigates stationary sound waves in cylindrical tubes. Fig. 1.1 shows a stationary wave pattern in a tube which is open at both ends. The tube has length L and diameter d. The frequency of the sound for the stationary wave pattern shown is f. There are a number of different tubes available. It is suggested that the relationship between f and d is f = v / (2L + kd) where v is the speed of sound in air and k is a constant. Design a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between f and d. Explain how your results could be used to determine values for k and v. You should draw a diagram, on page 3, showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your account you should pay particular attention to: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: stationary sound waves in cylindrical tubes open at both ends
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: d is the independent variable and f is the dependent variable.
Keep length L of the tube constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: tube supported; loudspeaker positioned in line with the tube; loudspeaker labelled.
Labelled microphone positioned outside the tube in line with the tube, connected to a labelled oscilloscope.
Adjust the frequency until maximum amplitude is detected.
Measure diameter d of the tube using calipers.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of 1 / f against d.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Use ear defenders or use a low volume to prevent damage to hearing.
Measure length L of the tube using a rule.
Increase frequency from a low frequency until the first maximum amplitude is detected.
To determine the value of f at maximum amplitude, increase the frequency to the maximum, then continue increasing it and then decrease it until the value of f is determined.
Use the oscilloscope to determine the period T, e.g. number of divisions × time-base.
Calculate f = 1 / T.
Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions, at different positions or along the tube and average.
Perform the experiment in a quiet room.
Show the signal generator connected to the loudspeaker in the diagram.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.

target_answer.response:
d is the independent variable and f is the dependent variable.
Keep length L of the tube constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• tube supported
• loudspeaker positioned in line with the tube
• loudspeaker labelled
• microphone positioned outside the tube in line with the tube
• microphone connected to an oscilloscope
Draw a circuit diagram with a signal generator connected to the loudspeaker and the microphone connected to the oscilloscope.

Measure length L of the tube using a rule.
Measure diameter d of the tube using calipers. Repeat measurements of diameter in different directions, at different positions or along the tube and average.
Increase frequency from a low frequency until the first maximum amplitude is detected. To determine the value of f at maximum amplitude, increase the frequency to the maximum, then continue increasing it and then decrease it until the value of f is determined.
Use the oscilloscope to determine the period T, for example using number of divisions × time-base. Calculate f = 1 / T.
Perform the experiment in a quiet room.

Plot a graph of 1 / f against d.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.
v = 2L / y-intercept.
k = gradient × v.

Safety:
Use ear defenders or use a low volume to prevent damage to hearing.

question.raw_question_text: A student investigates the extension of a spring supporting a wooden strip, as shown in Fig. 1.1. One end of the strip is at point P. The strip has length L and is at an angle θ to the bench. The spring is attached to the strip by a wire at a distance d from point P. The wire is at an angle β to the strip. The spring has extension x. It is suggested that the relationship between x and θ is WL/2 cos θ = kxd sin β where k is the spring constant of the spring and W is a constant. Design a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between x and θ. Explain how your results could be used to determine a value for W. You should draw a diagram, on page 3, showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your account you should pay particular attention to: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: extension of a spring supporting a wooden strip
question.given_relationship: WL/2 cos θ = kxd sin β
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: θ is the independent variable and x is the dependent variable.
Keep angle β constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: spring attached at both ends, with one end connected to a clamp and stand; strip free to move; at least two labels from clamp, stand, wire, strip, spring and bench.
Measure length L of the strip and distance d from point P using a rule.
Measure angle θ using a protractor.
Measure the original length of the spring and the new length of the spring using a rule or calipers.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of x against cos θ.
Relationship valid if a straight line passing through the origin is produced.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Wear goggles to prevent the spring, wire or strip entering the eyes, or clamp the stand to the bench.
Keep distance d constant.
Use a separate experiment to determine the spring constant k.
Determine k using k = weight / extension, k = mg / extension or the gradient of a weight–extension graph.
Prevent the strip at point P from sliding, for example using adhesive putty or a hinge, but allow rotation.
Use fiducial markers on the spring at both ends or measure the spring length on both sides and average.
Attach the wire to the strip by wrapping the wire around the strip, using strong tape or drilling a hole and tying the wire.
Determine x by subtracting the original spring length from the new spring length.
Adjust the support of the spring to keep angle β constant.
Position the protractor correctly to measure θ, or use a correct trigonometric relationship to determine θ.

target_answer.response:
θ is the independent variable and x is the dependent variable.
Keep length L of the strip, distance d from point P and angle β constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• spring attached at both ends, with one end connected to a clamp and stand
• strip free to move about point P
• labels for the clamp, stand, wire, strip, spring and bench
Use adhesive putty or a hinge at point P to prevent the strip from sliding but allow rotation.
Attach the wire to the strip by wrapping the wire around the strip, using strong tape or drilling a hole and tying the wire.

Measure length L of the strip and distance d from point P using a rule.
Measure angle θ using a protractor. Position the protractor correctly to measure θ.
Measure the original length of the spring and the new length of the spring using a rule or calipers. Use fiducial markers on the spring at both ends or measure the spring length on both sides and average. Determine x by subtracting the original spring length from the new spring length.
Adjust the support of the spring to keep angle β constant.
Use a separate experiment to determine the spring constant k. Determine k using k = weight / extension, k = mg / extension or the gradient of a weight–extension graph.

Plot a graph of x against cos θ.
Relationship valid if a straight line passing through the origin is produced.
W = gradient × 2kd sin β / L.

Safety:
Wear goggles to prevent the spring, wire or strip entering the eyes, or clamp the stand to the bench.

question.raw_question_text: A trolley with a magnet attached is placed on a thin steel sheet. The angle between the sheet and the bench is θ. The distance from point X to the trolley is d. The trolley is released from rest and travels down the slope. The velocity v of the trolley at X is determined using a light gate. It is suggested that the relationship between v and θ is mp sin θ − qB = mv^2 / 2d, where m is the mass of the trolley and magnet, B is the magnetic flux density between the magnet and the steel sheet, and p and q are constants. Design a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between v and θ. Explain how your results could be used to determine values for p and q. You should draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: trolley with a magnet moving down a steel sheet inclined at an angle θ
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: θ is the independent variable and v is the dependent variable.
Keep d constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: sheet supported by a stand or jack; light gate positioned at X; support, light gate and X labelled.
Connect the light gate to a timer or datalogger.
Measure the length L of the card interrupted by the beam for a single light gate.
Method to measure θ, e.g. use a protractor or determine θ from two measured lengths using a trigonometric ratio.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of v^2 against sin θ.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Place a block or stop on the bench near the end of the sheet to stop the trolley after it passes X.
Keep magnetic flux density B and mass m constant.
Measure d using a rule.
Keep d constant by marking the distance d on the sheet or marking the starting position of the trolley on the sheet.
Measure the mass of the trolley and magnet using a balance or by measuring the weight with a newton meter and dividing by g. Measure B using a calibrated Hall probe.
For the Hall probe, adjust the probe until the maximum reading is obtained or measure in opposite directions and average.
Determine v at X using v = L / t for a single light gate.
Include additional detail on measuring θ, e.g. draw the protractor in the correct position on the diagram or state the trigonometric relationship used.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.
Repeat experiment for each value of θ and average v.

target_answer.response:
θ is the independent variable and v is the dependent variable.
Keep distance d from point X to the trolley, mass m of the trolley and magnet, and magnetic flux density B constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• steel sheet supported by a stand or jack
• light gate positioned at X
• labels for the support, light gate and X
Connect the light gate to a timer or datalogger.

Measure the length L of the card interrupted by the beam for a single light gate. Determine v at X using v = L / t.
Measure θ using a protractor, or measure two appropriate lengths and determine θ using a trigonometric ratio. Draw the protractor in the correct position on the diagram if it is used.
Measure d using a rule. Keep d constant by marking the distance d on the sheet or by marking the starting position of the trolley on the sheet.
Measure the mass of the trolley and magnet using a balance or by measuring the weight with a newton meter and dividing by g. Measure B using a calibrated Hall probe. Adjust the Hall probe until the maximum reading is obtained, or measure in opposite directions and average.
Place a block or stop on the bench near the end of the sheet to stop the trolley after it passes X.

Repeat experiment for each value of θ and average v.
Plot a graph of v^2 against sin θ.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.
p = gradient / 2d.
q = -(m × y-intercept) / 2Bd.

Safety:
Use a block or stop to prevent the trolley leaving the sheet.

question.raw_question_text: Two parallel metal plates, each of area A, are separated by a small distance d. The plates are initially charged using a power supply. The plates are then connected to an uncharged capacitor. The potential difference V across the capacitor is measured. It is suggested that V is related to d by the relationship V = W / (1 + Cd / KA), where C is the capacitance of the capacitor, and K and W are constants. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between V and d. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for K and W. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: two parallel metal plates connected to a capacitor after being charged
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: d is the independent variable and V is the dependent variable.
Keep area A of overlap of the plates constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: voltmeter connected in parallel with the capacitor; capacitor and voltmeter connected to the metal plates with no power supply in the discharge part of the circuit; correct symbols for capacitor and voltmeter.
Method to charge the parallel plates, e.g. a separate charging circuit with a d.c. power supply or a combined circuit with switches and a d.c. power supply.
Measure d using calipers or measure the thickness of spacers using a micrometer or calipers.
Measure lengths to determine A and calculate A = length × breadth.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of 1 / V against d.

additional_detail_pool.point_text: Use gloves to prevent electric shock or do not touch the metal plates.
Keep the initial potential difference across the plates, or the initial charge, constant.
Determine the value of C using a separate experiment, e.g. measure potential difference or current against time during discharge through a resistor.
Describe the operation of the circuit using switches.
Fully discharge the capacitor between experiments, e.g. short-circuit the capacitor or use a switch in parallel with the capacitor.
Repeat measurements of d at different points across the plates and average.
Repeat measurements of V for the same d and average V.
Rest the bottom plate on insulating material or support the top plate by strings.
Use a high-voltage power supply to increase the charge on the plates or use a very small capacitance to increase the voltmeter reading.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.

target_answer.response:
d is the independent variable and V is the dependent variable.
Keep capacitance C of the capacitor and area A of overlap of the plates constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• voltmeter connected in parallel with the capacitor
• capacitor and voltmeter connected to the metal plates with no power supply in the discharge part of the circuit
• correct symbols for the capacitor and voltmeter.
Draw a charging circuit for the plates using a d.c. power supply, for example a separate charging circuit or a combined circuit with switches and the d.c. power supply.

Measure d using calipers, or use spacers and measure the thickness of the spacers with a micrometer or calipers. Repeat measurements of d at different points across the plates and average.
Measure the plate length and breadth and calculate A = length × breadth.
Determine the value of C using a separate experiment, for example by measuring potential difference or current against time during discharge through a resistor.
Use switches to operate the charging and discharge circuits. Fully discharge the capacitor between experiments by short-circuiting it or by using a switch in parallel with the capacitor.
Keep the initial potential difference across the plates, or the initial charge on the plates, constant. Rest the bottom plate on insulating material or support the top plate by strings. Use a high-voltage power supply to increase the charge on the plates, or use a very small capacitance to increase the voltmeter reading.

Repeat measurements of V for the same d and average V.
Plot a graph of 1 / V against d.
Relationship valid if a straight line not passing through the origin is produced.
K = C / (A × W × gradient).
W = 1 / y-intercept.

Safety:
Use gloves to prevent electric shock or do not touch the metal plates.

question.raw_question_text: Two parallel cylindrical conductors each have a small cross-sectional area A. A thin metal bar connects the two conductors. The metal bar has a square cross-section with sides of length y. For each conductor, the distance between its end C and the centre of the metal bar is L. The distance between the centres of the conductors is x. The ends C are connected to a power supply and the current I in the conductors is measured. It is suggested that I is related to L by the relationship I = E / (2PL / A + Qx / y^2), where E is the electromotive force of the power supply, and P and Q are constants. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between I and L. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for P and Q. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: current in two parallel cylindrical conductors connected by a thin metal bar
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: L is the independent variable and I is the dependent variable.
Keep E constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: circuit diagram with power supply connected to ends C; ammeter in series with the power supply and conductors; correct symbols for the ammeter and power supply.
Circuit diagram with a voltmeter correctly positioned to measure E across the power supply.
Use a rule to measure L and x.

Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between z and V. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for a and b. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.

focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

question.raw_question_text: Fig. 1.1 shows a thin cylindrical metal rod of length L. One end of the rod is hit with a hammer. A stationary sound wave is set up within the rod. The rod vibrates at its resonant frequency f. A microphone placed at the other end of the rod detects the sound wave emitted from the rod. The frequency of the detected sound is also f. A number of rods of different length are available. It is suggested that f is related to L by the relationship 2fL^n = √(E / ρ) where ρ is the density of the metal, and E and n are constants. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between f and L. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for E and n. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.

Measure length L of the rod using a metre rule.
Measure mass m of the metal rod using a top-pan balance.

Repeat measurements of f for each value of L and average f.

Measure mass m of the metal rod using a top-pan balance. Measure diameter d of the rod using a micrometer or calipers. Repeat measurements at different positions or in different directions and average. Calculate A = πd^2 / 4 and determine density using ρ = m / (AL).

question.raw_question_text: Fig. 1.1 shows a small solid metal cylinder of mass m, length L and diameter d. The cylinder is heated to a uniform temperature. The cylinder is then removed from the heat source and the cylinder is wrapped in an insulating material. The temperature of the room is TR. At time t after the cylinder starts to cool, the surface temperature of the cylinder is TC. It is suggested that TC is related to t by the relationship (TC - TR) = Z e^(-UAt / mc) where A is the total surface area of the cylinder, c is the specific heat capacity of the metal, and U and Z are constants. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between TC and t. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for U and Z. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
question.experiment_context: cooling of a heated solid metal cylinder wrapped in insulating material
focus_points_from_question: any safety precautions to be taken

defining_problem.point_text: t is the independent variable and TC is the dependent variable.
Keep room temperature TR constant.

methods_of_data_collection.point_text: Labelled diagram of workable experiment including: solid cylinder cooling; insulation surrounding all of the cylinder; thermometer touching the cylinder inside the insulation; insulation and thermometer labelled.
Method to heat the cylinder uniformly, for example place it in an oven or immerse it in hot water.
Determine time t using a stopwatch or a temperature sensor connected to a data logger.
Measure length L using a ruler, calipers or micrometer, and measure diameter d using calipers or micrometer.

method_of_analysis.point_text: Plot a graph of ln (TC - TR) against t.
Relationship valid if a straight line with y-intercept = ln Z is produced.

Keep the thickness of the insulating material constant.
Measure mass m using a top-pan balance.
For an oven or hot-water method, wait until the initial temperature of the cylinder is uniform throughout the cylinder.
Calculate the total surface area using A = πdL + πd^2 / 2.
Repeat measurements of d along the cylinder or in different directions and average d.
Determine c in a separate experiment by heating the cylinder electrically and using c = ΔE / (mΔθ).
Determine ΔE with a joulemeter or by measuring current, potential difference and heating time and calculating IVt.
Use several temperature sensors and average TC.

t is the independent variable and TC is the dependent variable.
Keep room temperature TR constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:
• solid cylinder cooling

• thermometer touching the cylinder inside the insulation
• labels for the insulation and thermometer
• a method to heat the cylinder uniformly, for example an oven or a hot-water bath.

Measure time t using a stopwatch or use temperature sensors connected to a data logger.
Measure length L using a ruler, calipers or micrometer, and measure diameter d using calipers or micrometer. Repeat measurements of d along the cylinder or in different directions and average. Calculate the total surface area using A = πdL + πd^2 / 2.
Measure mass m using a top-pan balance.
Wait until the initial temperature of the cylinder is uniform throughout the cylinder before starting the cooling. Keep the thickness of the insulating material constant. Use several temperature sensors and average TC if available.
Determine c in a separate experiment by heating the cylinder electrically and using c = ΔE / (mΔθ). Determine ΔE with a joulemeter or by measuring current, potential difference and heating time and calculating IVt.

question.raw_question_text: A spring is attached to a strong cylindrical magnet of length L and cross-sectional area A. The magnet is placed on thin card on top of a magnetic sheet on the bench, as shown in Fig. 1.1. The thickness of the card is t. The magnetic flux density at one of the poles of the magnet is B. A force is applied upwards to the spring. The extension of the spring when the magnet just leaves the card is s. It is suggested that s is related to t by the relationship ks = ALBZ / t where k is the spring constant of the spring and Z is a constant. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between s and t. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. Explain how the results could be used to determine a value for Z. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.

defining_problem.point_text: t is the independent variable and s is the dependent variable.
Keep spring constant k constant.

Determine extension using s = new length or position of the spring - original length or position of the spring.
Measure thickness t of the card using a micrometer.
Measure magnetic flux density B using a calibrated Hall probe and rotate the probe until the maximum reading is obtained, or measure in opposite directions and average.

Use pointers or markers on the spring to read values from the rule.
Use video recording and replay to determine the maximum spring length, or increase the force gradually until the magnet just leaves the card.
Repeat measurements of t at different positions on the card and average t, or repeat measurements of s for each value of t and average s.
Check that the spring has not exceeded its elastic limit.
Use a non-magnetic stand, for example wood.

t is the independent variable and s is the dependent variable.
Keep magnetic flux density B, length L and cross-sectional area A of the magnet constant.

Labelled diagram of workable experiment including:

• vertical rule parallel to the spring to determine s
• rule held in position by a stand
• stand resting on the bench
• labels for the rule and at least one other item from stand, clamp, card, magnetic sheet, magnet or spring.

Measure thickness t of the card using a micrometer. Repeat measurements at different positions on the card and average t.

Repeat measurements of s for each value of t and average s.
Plot a graph of s against 1 / t.

Z = gradient × k / (ALB).

question.raw_question_text: A thin cylindrical bar magnet of length L and cross-sectional area A is attached to a block. An identical magnet is attached to a trolley, as shown in Fig. 1.1. The trolley is held so that the separation of the N poles of the two magnets is s. Point P is a distance D from the N pole of the magnet on the stationary trolley. The trolley is released. The speed v of the trolley at point P is determined using one light gate. It is suggested that v is related to s by the relationship Dmv^2 / 2 = KA^2B^2L^2 / s^4 - Q where B is the magnetic flux density at the N pole of one of the magnets, m is the mass of the trolley, and K and Q are constants. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between v and s. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for K and Q. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.

Use strong magnets to increase v.
Repeat measurements of v for each value of s and average v.

• at least one other label from block, magnets, trolley, s or D.

question.raw_question_text: Fig. 1.1 shows a coil made from resistance wire. The coil is placed in cooking oil of mass m. The total length of the resistance wire in the oil is L. A potential difference V is applied to the coil. The temperature of the oil increases by Δθ in time t. It is suggested that Δθ is related to L by the relationship AtV^2 / L = mKΔθ + Z where A is the cross-sectional area of the wire, and K and Z are constants. Plan a laboratory experiment to test the relationship between Δθ and L. Draw a diagram showing the arrangement of your equipment. Explain how the results could be used to determine values for K and Z. In your plan you should include: the procedure to be followed, the measurements to be taken, the control of variables, the analysis of the data, any safety precautions to be taken.
